title: Progressive Central Precocious Puberty with Coincidental Primary Hypothyroidism
subtitle: A Resident Training Module: MRI indications, GnRHa timing, and the Van Wyk-Grumbach trap
authors: Resident training module — illustrative teaching case

patient.chiefComplaint: Menarche at 8.5 years, thelarche at 7 years, rapid growth velocity.
pmh: Thelarche started at 7 years of age
Menarche occurred at 8.5 years of age

caseDescription: An 8-year-6-month-old girl presents with menarche and a history of thelarche beginning at 7 years, accompanied by rapid growth over the past 1.5 years. Her weight is 32 kg. Lab results show: TSH 7.4 mIU/L, Total T4 4.75 mcg/dL (lower limit of normal is 5.1, consistent with mild clinical hypothyroidism). Gonadotropins and sex steroids: FSH 2.77 IU/L, LH 2.87 IU/L (pubertal level), Estradiol 91 pg/mL (significantly elevated), Testosterone 0.09 ng/mL, 17-OHP awaited. Bone age is 9 years (mildly advanced). The clinical dilemma centers around whether this is simply primary hypothyroidism causing early puberty (Van Wyk-Grumbach syndrome) or true progressive central precocious puberty with coincidental hypothyroidism, and the subsequent decisions regarding brain MRI, GnRH stimulation, and GnRH agonist therapy.
keyFindings: Menarche at 8.5 years, thelarche at 7 years
Frankly pubertal gonadotropins (LH 2.87) and high estradiol (91 pg/mL)
finalDiagnosis: Progressive central precocious puberty (CPP) + mild primary hypothyroidism (likely Hashimoto’s). This is not classic Van Wyk-Grumbach syndrome because LH is not suppressed and bone age is advanced rather than delayed.
learningObjectives: Differentiate true Central Precocious Puberty with coincidental hypothyroidism from Van Wyk-Grumbach syndrome.
Identify absolute indications for MRI Brain/Sella in girls with progressive CPP.
Formulate a sequenced management plan for coexisting CPP and primary hypothyroidism (LT4 vs GnRHa initiation).
Calculate starting levothyroxine doses in pubertal pediatric patients.

items.title: Why this is NOT Van Wyk-Grumbach Syndrome
items.content: A common resident trap is attributing any early pubertal development in the presence of elevated TSH to Van Wyk-Grumbach (VWG) syndrome. Classic VWG is characterized by severe, long-standing primary hypothyroidism leading to delayed bone age, suppressed LH/FSH (due to high prolactin/lack of GnRH pulsatility), and ovarian cysts formed by profound TSH cross-reactivity at the FSH receptor. In contrast, this patient has mild primary hypothyroidism (TSH only 7.4), frankly pubertal LH (2.87), significantly elevated estradiol (91 pg/mL), and an advanced bone age (9 years). This profile unequivocally indicates true, progressive Central Precocious Puberty (CPP) with coincidental mild primary hypothyroidism.
keyPoints: VWG features: profound hypothyroidism, delayed bone age, suppressed LH.
This patient features: mild hypothyroidism, advanced bone age, pubertal LH.
Diagnosis: True progressive CPP + coincidental hypothyroidism.

items.title: Why MRI Sella/Brain is mandatory despite the 8-year 'grey zone'
items.content: Even though the patient is 8 years 6 months old (often considered a 'grey zone' where some isolated early puberty might not universally mandate imaging), the clinical picture dictates an urgent MRI Brain with pituitary protocol and contrast. The rationale: she had thelarche at ~7 years, cycles at 8.5 years, rapid growth velocity, pubertal LH, and high estradiol. This represents rapid, progressive CPP. Furthermore, the coexisting primary hypothyroidism, even if mild, increases the necessity to definitively rule out sellar/suprasellar pathology (e.g., craniopharyngioma, hypothalamic hamartoma, or infiltrative disease). An MRI is an absolute must and should not be skipped.
keyPoints: Thelarche at 7y + rapid progression to menarche at 8.5y = high suspicion for organic cause.
Coexisting hypothyroidism amplifies the need to exclude structural sellar/suprasellar lesions.
A contrast-enhanced MRI with pituitary protocol is strongly indicated.

items.title: Why a GnRH stimulation test adds nothing here
items.content: In many modern pediatric endocrine centers, a formal GnRH stimulation test is no longer mandatory when the clinical and biochemical picture is already unambiguous. With a basal LH of 2.87 IU/L, a significantly elevated estradiol of 91 pg/mL, and clear clinical progression (Tanner stage B5, menarche), the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis is demonstrably active. A stimulation test will not change management; it will only confirm what the basal labs and physical exam already prove. The focus should shift to identifying the etiology (MRI) and initiating treatment.
keyPoints: Basal LH > 0.3-0.5 IU/L (assay dependent) often confirms pubertal activation.
Unequivocal clinical progression + pubertal basal labs obviate the need for stimulation testing.
Avoid unnecessary dynamic testing when it won't alter the management plan.

items.title: How to sequence and time GnRH agonist therapy (age >8 does not preclude it)
items.content: A common misconception is that a girl who has crossed her 8th birthday is no longer 'precocious' and therefore doesn't warrant GnRH agonist (GnRHa) therapy. However, this patient's puberty started at 7 years (thelarche), progressed rapidly to menarche by 8.5 years, and is driving bone age advancement. Without intervention, her epiphyses will fuse prematurely, severely compromising her predicted adult height. GnRHa therapy (e.g., Leuprolide depot) is clearly indicated. The strategic sequence: (1) Order MRI and start levothyroxine, (2) Wait for MRI clearance, (3) Initiate GnRHa. Treating the mild hypothyroidism alone will not arrest the robustly activated HPG axis.
keyPoints: Age > 8 years at presentation does not preclude treatment if onset was < 8 years and progression is rapid.
GnRHa is essential to halt bone age advancement and preserve adult height.
Sequence: MRI + LT4 first, followed rapidly by GnRHa once structural lesions are managed/excluded.

items.title: Levothyroxine Dosing and Monitoring
items.content: For a 32 kg girl with primary hypothyroidism, the starting dose of levothyroxine is approximately 2.3-2.4 mcg/kg/day. A practical starting dose is 75 mcg once daily (or 88 mcg if 75 mcg is unavailable). Baseline autoimmune markers (TPO and Tg antibodies) should be drawn to confirm the likely etiology of Hashimoto's thyroiditis. Follow-up strategy: Recheck TSH and free T4 in 6-8 weeks. The target is a normalized TSH (ideally 0.5-2.5 mIU/L) with T4 in the mid-to-upper normal range. Once euthyroidism is achieved, the tempo of puberty must be continually reassessed, though in this case, the GnRHa will be doing the heavy lifting to arrest pubertal progression.
keyPoints: Pediatric LT4 dose varies by age/weight; ~2-3 mcg/kg/day is appropriate for this age.
Start 75 mcg daily for a 32 kg patient.
Target TSH 0.5-2.5 mIU/L; monitor every 6-8 weeks until stable.

items.question: What three features in this patient's presentation distinguish her condition from classic Van Wyk-Grumbach syndrome?
items.answer: 1) Mild (rather than profound) TSH elevation (7.4 mIU/L). 2) Frankly pubertal, unsuppressed LH (2.87 IU/L). 3) Advanced bone age (9 years) rather than the delayed bone age characteristic of long-standing severe hypothyroidism.

items.question: Why is an MRI Brain/Sella considered mandatory for this 8.5-year-old girl?
items.answer: Despite being >8 years old currently, her onset of thelarche was at 7 years, she has had rapid progression to menarche, and her labs show robust central activation. This rapid tempo, combined with coexisting primary hypothyroidism, raises a significant red flag for structural sellar/suprasellar pathology (like a craniopharyngioma) that must be excluded with a contrast-enhanced MRI.

items.question: Calculate the approximate starting dose of levothyroxine for this 32 kg girl.
items.answer: The recommended dose is approximately 2.3-2.4 mcg/kg/day. 32 kg x 2.3 mcg/kg = 73.6 mcg. A practical and appropriate starting dose is 75 mcg once daily.

items.question: Why is a GnRH stimulation test not strictly necessary in this patient?
items.answer: A GnRH stimulation test is used to prove central activation when basal labs are equivocal. In this patient, the basal LH is already clearly pubertal (2.87 IU/L), estradiol is significantly elevated (91 pg/mL), and she has undeniable clinical evidence of advanced puberty (menarche, rapid growth). Dynamic testing would add cost and discomfort without altering the diagnosis or management plan.

items.question: How should the initiation of Levothyroxine and GnRH agonist be sequenced in this patient?
items.answer: 1) Immediately order an MRI brain/sella with contrast and initiate Levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. 2) Check autoimmune thyroid markers (TPO, TgAb). 3) Once the MRI is completed (to rule out lesions requiring surgical intervention) and thyroid treatment has started, initiate the GnRH agonist (e.g., Leuprolide depot) to halt pubertal progression and preserve adult height.

items.stem: An 8.5-year-old girl presents with menarche. She had thelarche at age 7. Labs show TSH 7.4, LH 2.87, and Estradiol 91 pg/mL. Bone age is 9 years. Which of the following is the most accurate diagnosis?
options: Van Wyk-Grumbach syndrome
Premature thelarche variant
Progressive Central Precocious Puberty with coincidental primary hypothyroidism
Peripheral precocious puberty secondary to an ovarian cyst
items.explanation: The patient has elevated LH indicating central activation, advanced bone age, and rapid progression, which defines CPP. The mildly elevated TSH indicates coincidental primary hypothyroidism. VWG would have suppressed LH and delayed bone age. Peripheral puberty would have suppressed LH.

items.stem: Which of the following interventions is NOT indicated in the initial management plan for this patient?
options: MRI Brain with pituitary protocol and contrast
Initiation of Levothyroxine at ~2.3 mcg/kg/day
Formal GnRH stimulation testing
Testing for TPO and Tg antibodies
items.explanation: Formal GnRH stimulation testing is not indicated because the basal LH (2.87 IU/L) is already clearly in the pubertal range, and clinical progression is unequivocal. The other options are all mandatory/strongly indicated steps in her management.

items.statement: Because the patient is currently 8 years and 6 months old, she is no longer considered 'precocious' and should not receive GnRH agonist therapy.
items.explanation: False. Although she is currently >8 years old, her puberty began at age 7 (thelarche). The rapid progression to menarche by 8.5 years and the advancing bone age threaten her final adult height. GnRH agonist therapy is indicated to halt this progression.

items.assertion: An MRI Brain/Sella is strongly indicated in this 8.5-year-old girl.
items.reason: The rapid progression from thelarche at 7 to menarche at 8.5, combined with coexisting primary hypothyroidism, creates a high suspicion for an organic structural lesion.
items.explanation: Both the Assertion and the Reason are correct, and the Reason explains the Assertion. The combination of early onset, rapid tempo, and multiple endocrine axis involvement (thyroid and gonadal) mandates ruling out central pathology.

items.stem: Which three features in the patient's presentation distinguish her condition from classic Van Wyk-Grumbach syndrome?
options: A) Mild TSH elevation, pubertal LH, advanced bone age
B) Profound TSH elevation, suppressed LH, delayed bone age
C) Normal TSH, elevated LH, advanced bone age
D) Mild TSH elevation, suppressed LH, delayed bone age
items.explanation: Van Wyk-Grumbach syndrome typically presents with profound hypothyroidism, suppressed gonadotropins, and delayed bone age. The patient's mild TSH elevation, unsuppressed LH, and advanced bone age contrast with these features.

items.stem: What is the approximate starting dose of levothyroxine for a 32 kg girl?
options: A. 70 mcg once daily
B. 75 mcg once daily
C. 80 mcg once daily
D. 100 mcg once daily
items.explanation: The recommended pediatric dose is 2.3-2.4 mcg/kg/day. 32 kg × 2.3 mcg/kg = 73.6 mcg, which rounds to 75 mcg as a practical starting dose. Options A, C, and D represent either miscalculations or adult doses.

items.stem: Which of the following is the most appropriate reason to forgo a GnRH stimulation test in this patient?
options: A) The patient has a history of negative prior testing
B) Basal LH and estradiol levels are already consistent with central puberty, and clinical findings confirm advanced pubertal development
C) The patient prefers non-invasive diagnostic approaches
D) The test is contraindicated due to a known allergy to GnRH analogs
items.explanation: Basal LH (2.87 IU/L) and estradiol (91 pg/mL) levels, along with clinical evidence of menarche and rapid growth, confirm central puberty without needing dynamic testing. The test would not alter management and adds unnecessary cost and discomfort.

items.stem: What is the appropriate sequence for initiating Levothyroxine and GnRH agonist in a patient with suspected hypothalamic-pituitary dysfunction and thyroid abnormalities?
options: A) Perform MRI brain/sella with contrast first, start Levothyroxine 75 mcg daily, then initiate GnRH agonist after confirming no surgically urgent lesions
B) Start GnRH agonist immediately to halt puberty, then perform MRI and initiate Levothyroxine
C) Check thyroid function tests first, then start GnRH agonist while awaiting MRI results
D) Initiate both Levothyroxine and GnRH agonist simultaneously without imaging
items.explanation: MRI must precede GnRH agonist initiation to exclude surgically urgent lesions; thyroid hormone replacement should begin before GnRH agonist to prevent complications from untreated hypothyroidism.
